items.text: Your credit report decides whether you get the loan and at what rate, so the law over it really answers two questions: who's allowed to pull your file, and what power you have to fix it when it's wrong. A lender can't open your report on a whim — it needs a permissible purpose, such as your own application for credit. And the score that lender sees isn't a black box: a FICO score is built from five weighted pieces — payment history at 35%, amounts owed at 30%, length of credit history at 15%, credit mix at 10%, and new credit and inquiries at 10% — so the single biggest lever, payment history, carries 35% of the score.

When you dispute an item, the furnisher and the bureau owe you an investigation on a clock — 30 days from receipt, with a 15-day extension if they genuinely need it, which pushes the outside limit to 45 days. Separately, after a mortgage application the lender has to hand you the credit-score disclosure quickly — within 3 business days. So if you apply on a Monday, that score disclosure is due within 3 business days.

Derogatory items don't sit on your file forever; each kind ages off on its own schedule. Most negatives — late payments, collections, charge-offs, foreclosures — drop after about 7 years (late payments can linger to roughly 7½). Bankruptcy splits by chapter: a Chapter 13 stays 7 years, while the heavier Chapter 7 stays a full 10 years on your file. So the chapter that haunts a borrower the longest, Chapter 7, sticks for 10 years.
